text: We present the case of a robotic repair of a large flank hernia. He's a sixty-six year old male with history of open left nephrectomy, one year after a left partial nephrectomy due to cancer. This was done laparoscopically. He developed an incisional hernia two months after. He comes one year after to our office. As you can see in the CAT scan, there's a large left flank hernia.
The patient is positioned laterally for the robotic repair of a large flank hernia. The video shows the arrangement of the operating room and the placement of trocars for the procedure.

The surgeon initiates dissection of the preperitoneal space lateral to the semilunaris line, creating a flap that includes the transversus abdominis fascia without cutting the muscle. The hernia sac is carefully reduced using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. The hernia sac is entered and dissected free from lateral attachments, revealing an opening in the peritoneum with the small bowel protruding through a defect.

The surgeon performs photodissection to detach the external oblique fascia and enters the retroperitoneal space, revealing the psoas muscle and surrounding nerves. The defect is then closed using placation of the eventrated external oblique with a non-absorbable barb suture, employing multiple throws to secure the closure.

The surgeon identifies an internal hernia with the small bowel protruding through a mesenteric defect of the descending colon into the retroperitoneal space. Sharp dissection is performed to reduce the small bowel. The mesenteric defect is closed from the peritoneal side, followed by closure of the peritoneum to exclude the extraperitoneal space. A 20x15 cm heavy-weight polypropylene mesh is placed to cover the repair site.

The surgeon fixes the mesh in cardinal points using interrupted video-micro sutures. The mesh is trimmed to prevent folding and retraction. An additional trocar is inserted to place a drain, and the peritoneal flap is closed with running trio barb absorbable sutures.
The patient tolerates the procedure well. He goes into recovery and he was discharged on post-op day two. Drain was removed on post-op day seven. No complications reported within ninety days post-op.
